text:
An Historical Sketch of Babylon

In the pages of history, there is no city more glamorous than Babylon. Just hearing its name brings up visions of wealth and splendor. Its treasures of gold and jewels were fabulous. You might naturally picture such a wealthy city in a beautiful setting of tropical luxury, surrounded by rich natural resources like forests and mines. However, that wasn't the case. Babylon was located beside the Euphrates River in a flat, dry valley. It had no forests, no mines, and not even stone for building. It wasn't even located on a natural trade route. The rainfall wasn't even enough to raise crops.

Babylon is an outstanding example of humanity's ability to achieve great things using whatever means are available. All the resources supporting this large city were developed by people. All of its riches were man-made.

Babylon possessed just two natural resources: a fertile soil and water in the river. Using one of the greatest engineering accomplishments in history, Babylonian engineers diverted the waters from the river using dams and immense irrigation canals. These canals stretched far out across that arid valley, pouring life-giving waters over the fertile soil. This ranks among the first engineering feats known to history. The world had never before seen such abundant crops as the reward of this irrigation system.

Fortunately, during its long existence, Babylon was ruled by successive lines of kings to whom conquest and plunder were only an afterthought. While it fought in many wars, most of these were local or defensive. They fought against ambitious conquerors from other countries who wanted Babylon's fabulous treasures. The outstanding rulers of Babylon live on in history because of their wisdom, enterprise, and justice. Babylon produced no arrogant monarchs who wanted to conquer the known world just so all nations could stroke their egos.

As a city, Babylon exists no more. When those energizing human forces that built and maintained the city for thousands of years were withdrawn, it quickly became a deserted ruin. The site of the city is in Asia, about six hundred miles east of the Suez Canal, just north of the Persian Gulf. Its latitude is about thirty degrees above the Equator, practically the same as Yuma, Arizona. It possessed a hot and dry climate very similar to that American city.

Today, this valley of the Euphrates, once a heavily populated irrigated farming district, is again a wind-swept dry wasteland. Scant grass and desert shrubs struggle for existence against the windblown sands. Gone are the fertile fields, the giant cities, and the long caravans of rich merchandise. Nomadic bands of Arabs, making a meager living by tending small herds, are the only inhabitants. It has been this way since about the beginning of the Christian era.

Earthen hills dot this valley. For centuries, travelers considered them to be nothing more than natural hills. Eventually, archaeologists noticed them because occasional rainstorms washed down broken pieces of pottery and brick. Expeditions financed by European and American museums were sent there to dig and see what they could find. Picks and shovels soon proved these hills to be ancient cities. You might well call them city graves.

Babylon was one of these cities. The desert dust had scattered over it for something like twenty centuries. Originally built of brick, all the exposed walls had disintegrated and gone back to the earth. Such is Babylon today—the once wealthy city is now a heap of dirt. It was abandoned for so long that no living person even knew its name until it was discovered by carefully removing centuries of refuse from its streets and the fallen wreckage of its noble temples and palaces.

Many scientists consider the civilization of Babylon and other cities in this valley to be the oldest civilization on definite record. Positive dates have been proven reaching back 8,000 years. An interesting fact is how they figured out these dates. In the ruins of Babylon, they uncovered descriptions of a solar eclipse. Modern astronomers easily calculated exactly when such an eclipse would have been visible in Babylon, establishing a known relationship between their calendar and our own.

In this way, we have proved that 8,000 years ago, the Sumerians, who inhabited Babylonia, were already living in walled cities. We can only guess how many centuries those cities had existed before that. The inhabitants were not mere barbarians living behind walls; they were an educated and enlightened people. As far as written history goes, they were the first engineers, the first astronomers, the first mathematicians, the first financiers, and the first people to have a written language.

We have already mentioned the irrigation systems that transformed the dry valley into an agricultural paradise. You can still trace the remains of these canals today, although they are mostly filled with accumulated sand. Some of them were so huge that, when empty of water, a dozen horses could be ridden side-by-side along their bottoms. In size, they compare favorably with the largest canals in Colorado and Utah.

In addition to irrigating the valley lands, Babylonian engineers completed another massive project. Using an elaborate drainage system, they reclaimed a huge area of swamp land at the mouths of the Euphrates and Tigris Rivers, putting this land under cultivation as well.

Herodotus, the Greek traveler and historian, visited Babylon while it was in its prime. He gave us the only known description written by an outsider. His writings provide a graphic description of the city and some of the unusual customs of its people. He mentions the remarkable fertility of the soil and the bountiful harvests of wheat and barley they produced.

The glory of Babylon has faded, but its wisdom has been preserved for us. We are indebted to their form of records for this. Back then, paper hadn't been invented yet. Instead, they painstakingly engraved their writing upon tablets of moist clay. When finished, these were baked into hard tile. They were about six by eight inches in size, and an inch thick.

These clay tablets were used much like we use modern forms of writing. They engraved legends, poetry, history, royal decrees, the laws of the land, property titles, promissory notes, and even letters sent by messengers to distant cities. From these clay tablets, we get an insight into the intimate, personal affairs of the people. For example, one tablet from a country storekeeper's records states that a specific customer brought in a cow and exchanged it for seven sacks of wheat. Three sacks were delivered immediately, and the other four were kept waiting for the customer's pleasure.

Archaeologists have safely recovered entire libraries of these tablets from the wrecked cities—hundreds of thousands of them.

One of the outstanding wonders of Babylon was the immense walls surrounding the city. The ancients ranked them with the great pyramid of Egypt as one of the "seven wonders of the world." Queen Semiramis is credited with building the first walls during the early history of the city. Modern excavators haven't been able to find any trace of the original walls, and their exact height isn't known. From mentions by early writers, it is estimated they were about fifty to sixty feet high, faced on the outer side with burnt brick, and further protected by a deep moat of water.

The later, more famous walls were started about six hundred years before the time of Christ by King Nabopolassar. He planned the rebuilding on such a gigantic scale that he didn't live to see the work finished. This was left to his son, Nebuchadnezzar, whose name is familiar in Biblical history.

The height and length of these later walls stagger belief. Reliable authorities report they were about one hundred and sixty feet high—the equivalent of a modern fifteen-story office building. The total length is estimated between nine and eleven miles. The top was so wide that a six-horse chariot could be driven around them. Little remains of this tremendous structure today except portions of the foundations and the moat. Along with the ravages of the elements, the Arabs completed the destruction by quarrying the brick for building purposes elsewhere.

The victorious armies of almost every conqueror of that age marched against the walls of Babylon. A host of kings laid siege to Babylon, but always failed. Invading armies back then weren't something to take lightly. Historians speak of units like 10,000 horsemen, 25,000 chariots, and 1,200 regiments of foot soldiers with 1,000 men in each regiment. It often took two or three years of preparation to assemble the war materials and food depots along the line of march.

The city of Babylon was organized much like a modern city. There were streets and shops. Peddlers offered their wares through residential districts. Priests officiated in magnificent temples. Inside the city was an inner enclosure for the royal palaces. The walls around the palaces were said to be even higher than the walls around the city itself.

The Babylonians were skilled in the arts. This included sculpture, painting, weaving, gold working, and manufacturing metal weapons and agricultural tools. Their jewelers created highly artistic jewelry. Many samples have been recovered from the graves of wealthy citizens and are now on exhibition in the world's leading museums.

At a very early period, when the rest of the world was still hacking at trees with stone-headed axes or hunting with flint-pointed spears, the Babylonians were already using axes, spears, and arrows with metal heads.

The Babylonians were clever financiers and traders. As far as we know, they were the original inventors of money as a means of exchange, promissory notes, and written titles to property.

Babylon was never entered by hostile armies until about 540 years before the birth of Christ. Even then, the walls were not captured. The story of Babylon's fall is very unusual. Cyrus, one of the great conquerors of that period, intended to attack the city and hoped to take its impregnable walls. However, the advisors of Nabonidus, the King of Babylon, persuaded him to go out and meet Cyrus in battle instead of waiting for a siege. In the resulting defeat of the Babylonian army, they fled away from the city. Cyrus then entered the open gates and took possession without resistance.

After that, the power and prestige of the city gradually faded. A few hundred years later, it was completely abandoned and deserted. It was left for the winds and storms to level once again back into the desert earth from which its grandeur had originally been built. Babylon had fallen, never to rise again, but civilization owes it a great debt.

Eons of time have crumbled the proud walls of its temples to dust, but the wisdom of Babylon endures.

Money is the medium by which earthly success is measured.

Money makes it possible to enjoy the best the earth has to offer.

Money is plentiful for those who understand the simple laws that govern its acquisition.

Today, money is governed by the exact same laws that controlled it when prosperous men thronged the streets of Babylon six thousand years ago.
